summary:
The drugs considered are essential treatments targeting specific mechanisms related to reducing excessive copper levels; among them, only \( \text{elemental } Ca^{} {2+} \) does not play a direct role in managing Wilson's disease symptoms or pathology compared to others that address core issues more effectively within this context.
The primary therapeutic agents include trientine, penicillamine, and zinc due to their proven efficacy against elevated intracellular/circulating copper concentrations characteristic of Wilson disease, whereas additional minerals such as calcium lack specificity towards correcting hepatic/liver dysfunction caused primarily by abnormal accumulation patterns involving metal ions predominantly handled via chelation therapies instead of mineral supplementation alone without added value here noted explicitly regarding pathophysiology management goals set forth clinically speaking concerning addressing root causes underlying most cases presenting acutely/chronically requiring intervention at various stages along progression trajectories observed longitudinally during follow-up evaluations performed regularly thereafter ensuring optimal long-term health maintenance plans customized per individual patient requirements identified early enough allowing timely interventions leading ultimately improved quality life expectancies achieved overall enhancing survival rates significantly when managed appropriately according guidelines recommended universally accepted standards governing care delivery systems worldwide dedicated solely improving lives impacted adversely otherwise potentially fatal consequences associated untreated severe forms left unchecked indefinitely
